1: "Jacob continued on his journey and came to the land of the eastern peoples" — bĕnê-qeḏem (sons/people of the east) is the biblical designation for the peoples of upper Mesopotamia and the Syrian steppe: the Aramaeans, and the inhabitants of the Haran/Paddan-Aram region. The phrase orients the reader geographically — Jacob has left the land of promise and entered the territory of his mother's extended family. The journey from Beersheba to Haran was approximately 450 miles, traveled alone.
2: "There he saw a well in the open country, with three flocks of sheep lying near it because the flocks were watered from that well. The stone over the mouth of the well was large" — a communal well shared by multiple flocks from the surrounding area was a standard feature of ANE pastoral life. The large stone covering the well mouth was a communal resource-management device: it prevented animals from falling in, reduced evaporation, and ensured that the well was only opened when the community gathered, preventing any single group from monopolizing the water. The detail that "three flocks" are waiting signals a community protocol governing shared access.
3: "When all the flocks were gathered there, the shepherds would roll the stone away from the well's mouth and water the sheep. Then they would return the stone to its place" — the protocol establishes that the stone was rolled communally (requiring coordinated effort), watering proceeded collectively, and the stone was replaced afterward. This was a fair-access system: no single flock could water before all others arrived. Jacob's single-handed rolling of the stone in Genesis 29:10, which would ordinarily require the combined effort of the assembled shepherds, marks his action as extraordinary.
4: "Jacob asked the shepherds, 'My brothers, where are you from?' 'We're from Haran'" — Jacob's opening address (ʾaḥay, my brothers) is the customary greeting between fellow shepherds and travelers in the ANE. The identification of Haran as their origin confirms Jacob is in the right region — Haran is the city where Terah settled and where Nahor's extended family remained (Genesis 11:31–32; Genesis 24:10).
5: "Do you know Laban, Nahor's grandson?" — Jacob identifies Laban through the genealogical line: Nahor (Abraham's brother) → Bethuel → Laban. In the ANE, identifying someone by lineage rather than by name alone was the standard social protocol; the family connection was the relevant social marker. The shepherds' affirmative response confirms Jacob has reached the right community.
6: "Here comes his daughter Rachel with the sheep" — the providential timing mirrors the servant's encounter with Rebekah in Genesis 24: the woman appears before the inquiry is complete. Rachel is introduced as a shepherdess (rōʿāh ṣōʾn ʾāḇîhā, tending her father's sheep) — a normal occupation for women in pastoral ANE societies. Zipporah and her sisters are shepherdesses in Exodus 2:16; the role was socially respectable for unmarried daughters in herding households.
7: "The sun is still high; it's not time for the flocks to be gathered" — Jacob's observation about the timing reveals his knowledge of shepherding customs. The gathering at the communal well typically happened at the end of the day, when the flocks were brought in from pasture. Watering in midday and releasing animals back to pasture would have been inefficient and unusual. Jacob's suggestion that the shepherds return the animals to pasture rather than waiting may be an attempt to be alone with Rachel when she arrives.
8: "We can't, until all the flocks are gathered and the stone has been rolled away from the well's mouth" — the shepherds' reply confirms the communal protocol of Genesis 29:3: the stone is a collective responsibility and cannot be moved until all parties are present. Their response is not laziness but adherence to the shared-access agreement. The explanation also sets up the contrast with Jacob's individual stone-rolling in Genesis 29:10.
9: "Rachel came with her father's flock, for she tended them" — the narrator's explanatory note (kî rōʿāh hîʾ, for she was a shepherdess) underscores that Rachel's presence at the well is occupational, not coincidental. She is not there because she happened to be passing; she is the responsible caretaker of Laban's flock. The scene parallels ch.Genesis 24:15-16, where Rebekah appeared at the well in her normal occupation (water-carrying) just as the servant prayed.
10: "He went over and rolled the stone away from the mouth of the well and watered his uncle's sheep" — Jacob's single-handed removal of the communal stone is a feat that would ordinarily require the collective effort of all the assembled shepherds (Genesis 29:3). The narrative offers no explanation for how he accomplished it — possibly indicating extraordinary strength, possibly that the three flocks' shepherds also helped when they saw his intent, or possibly a narrative signal of divine enabling. The sight of Rachel is the immediate cause: "when Jacob saw Rachel... he went over and rolled." The action bypasses the community protocol in an act of generous hospitality toward his kinsman's flock.
11: "Jacob kissed Rachel and began to weep aloud" — the kiss and weeping are standard ANE family-reunion gestures, not primarily romantic expressions at this stage. Genesis 45:14–15 (Joseph weeping on his brothers' necks), Genesis 33:4 (Esau running to kiss Jacob), and Genesis 46:29 (Joseph weeping on Israel's neck) all use the same elements — embrace, kiss, weeping — for family reunions after long separation. Jacob has traveled 450 miles alone; the sight of his kinswomen triggers the emotional release of arrival. The romantic dimension is present but secondary to the kinship reunion convention.
12: "He had told Rachel that he was a relative of her father and a son of Rebekah. So she ran and told her father" — Jacob identifies himself through both paternal kin connection (relative of Laban) and maternal line (son of Rebekah). Rebekah is the link — she is both Laban's sister and Jacob's mother. Rachel's running to tell her father mirrors Rebekah's running to tell her mother's household in Genesis 24:28 — both women function as messengers who activate the household's hospitality response.
13: "Laban heard the news about Jacob, his sister's son, he hurried to meet him. He embraced him and kissed him" — Laban's response to the news of a kinsman's arrival is appropriate ANE hospitality: the embrace (ḥābaqqô) and kiss (wĕnaššaq-lô) are the formal welcome gestures. Compare Laban's response in Genesis 24:29-30, where he also "hurried out" — but there, the text explicitly notes he was motivated by seeing gold jewelry on Rebekah. Here, only kinship is specified. Jacob has no gold; his reception is based on family connection alone.
14: "You are my own flesh and blood" (ʿaṣmî ûḇĕśārî ʾāttāh) — this is a formal covenant-kinship declaration, not mere sentiment. The formula ʿeṣem ûbāśār (bone and flesh) appears in Judges 9:2 (Shechem's citizens claiming kinship-obligation to Abimelech) and 2 Samuel 5:1 (Israel's tribes declaring covenant-kinship to David). Laban is formally acknowledging the kinship bond that creates mutual obligations. "After Jacob had stayed with him for a whole month" — a month of hospitality before business discussions reflects ANE social convention: guests were received before formal negotiations about labor or wages began.
15: "Just because you are a relative of mine, should you work for me for nothing?" — Laban's question opens the formal wage negotiation. In the ANE, a kinsman who worked within a household was not automatically exempt from wages simply because of the family connection; there was a recognized distinction between a guest being hosted and a worker providing ongoing labor. Laban's question formally acknowledges Jacob's work and invites Jacob to name what he wants — shifting the relationship from hospitality to economic exchange.
16: "Laban had two daughters; the name of the older was Leah, and the name of the younger was Rachel" — the narrator introduces the daughters with their birth-order prominently stated (older/younger). Birth order has been a recurring theme in Genesis — Cain/Abel, Ishmael/Isaac, Esau/Jacob — and the naming of the older before the younger here sets up the tension that will produce Laban's deception in Genesis 29:26. The names: Lēʾāh (possibly "wild cow" or "weary") and Rāḥēl (ewe/female sheep), both animal names common in the pastoral ANE world.
17: "Leah had soft eyes, but Rachel had a lovely figure and was beautiful" — Leah's eyes are described as rakkôt (soft, tender, delicate). The LXX translates this as "weak" in the sense of not impressive; some interpreters read "gentle" or "lovely" as a positive quality. The contrast with Rachel's beauty is clear regardless of the precise connotation. In the ANE honor-shame context, physical attractiveness was a social asset affecting marriage prospects, and the narrator's comparison establishes why Jacob's preference for Rachel is intelligible within ANE expectations.
18: "I'll work for you seven years in return for your younger daughter Rachel" — Jacob proposes bride service as the substitute for the mohar (bride price). In the ANE, a man without the resources to pay a bride price could substitute a period of labor service for his prospective father-in-law. Nuzi tablets from upper Mesopotamia (near Haran, 14th century BCE) document similar arrangements where a man without means would serve as an "adopted son" in the household and receive a daughter in marriage. Seven years is a round number suggesting completion and full payment — not an arbitrary period but the equivalent of a substantial bride price.
19: "It's better that I give her to you than to some other man" — Laban's agreement is framed as endogamous preference: better to keep the marriage within the kinship network than to give Rachel to an outsider. This reflects the ANE value of endogamy — marrying within the clan or tribe — which preserved family property and social networks. The response also subtly positions Laban as doing Jacob a favor, a framing that will serve Laban later when he claims the right to control the terms of the arrangement.
20: "They seemed like only a few days to him because of his love for her" — one of the most celebrated paradoxes in Genesis. The usual effect of time in labor is that it feels long; Jacob's love for Rachel inverts this. The phrase marks seven years as the background to what the narrative will cover in a single verse, yet makes the emotional weight of that period — love sustained through labor — palpable. The compressed timeline also sets up the shock of Genesis 29:25: after seven years of patient, love-driven service, the morning after the consummation reveals the deception.
21: "Give me my wife. My time is completed, and I want to make her my wife" — Jacob's demand is formal and contractual: the seven years are complete, the labor has been performed, the wage is due. "Give me my wife" (hāḇāh ʾet-ʾištu) uses the standard Hebrew idiom for completing a marriage transfer. Jacob has fulfilled his half of the agreement; he is now calling for Laban to fulfill his. The language is not romantic but legal — Jacob is claiming what the contract specifies.
22: "Laban brought together all the people of the place and gave a feast" — the wedding feast (mišteh) gathered the community (kol-ʾanšê hammāqôm). ANE wedding celebrations typically lasted seven days (Genesis 29:27: "finish this daughter's bridal week"); they were public community events, not private ceremonies. The community gathering is also relevant to the deception: with the community present, the substitution of Leah for Rachel would require community complicity or at least the kind of darkness and veiling that prevented recognition.
23: "But when evening came, he took his daughter Leah and brought her to Jacob, and Jacob made love to her" — the deception operates through the darkness and the bridal veil (cf. Genesis 24:65, where Rebekah veiled herself when she saw Isaac). ANE bridal practice required the bride to be veiled throughout the wedding feast and the first night; the man would not see her face until the following morning. The seven-day feast was presumably accompanied by wine, further reducing Jacob's capacity to detect the substitution. Laban's agency is clear: "he took... he brought" — Laban is the actor, not Leah.
24: (Laban gave his servant Zilpah to his daughter as her attendant.) — The narrator's parenthetical note about Zilpah anticipates her role in ch.Genesis 30:9-13, when Leah will give Zilpah to Jacob as a surrogate wife. Providing a maidservant as an attendant with the bride was an ANE marriage custom — the bride brought a domestic attendant who would serve her in her new household. Nuzi texts and other ANE sources confirm this practice; the attendant was understood as the bride's property within the new household.
25: "When morning came, there was Leah!" — the morning revelation is the narrative's hinge. The single sentence wĕhinnēh-hîʾ Lēʾāh (and behold, she was Leah) delivers the shock Jacob experiences. "What is this you have done to me?" — Jacob's complaint uses mah-zōʾt ʿāśîtā lî (what is this you have done to me), the same formula used by Pharaoh to Abraham (Genesis 12:18), Isaac to Abimelech (Genesis 26:10), and Abimelech to Jacob himself (Genesis 26) — a formula of moral accusation for a deception. The reader has already noted, in Genesis 27, that Jacob deceived his blind father using his brother's garments; here he is deceived by darkness and his wife's veil.
26: "It is not our custom here to give the younger daughter in marriage before the older one" — Laban's defense invokes social convention (kēn yēʿāśeh bimqōmēnû, thus it is done in our place). Birth-order priority in marriage was a recognized ANE custom, though not universal. Laban may have had this objection from the start but chose not to raise it when Jacob made his offer, instead waiting to exploit it at the moment of maximum vulnerability. The irony is precise: Jacob, who violated his own family's birth-order convention by stealing Esau's firstborn blessing, now finds the same convention invoked against him.
27: "Finish this daughter's bridal week; then we will give you the younger one also, in return for another seven years of work" — the "bridal week" (šĕbūaʿ zōʾt) is the seven-day wedding celebration period. Samson's wedding feast in Judges 14:12 is also a seven-day feast. The completion of Leah's wedding week before Rachel is given to Jacob was a social obligation — abandoning Leah's feast would have been a public disgrace. Laban's counter-offer requires another seven years of service — doubling Jacob's total obligation to fourteen years (7+7) in exchange for two wives.
28: "Jacob did so. He finished the week with Leah, and then Laban gave him his daughter Rachel to be his wife" — Jacob receives Rachel at the completion of Leah's wedding week, not at the completion of the second seven years. This is an important detail: Jacob is given both wives before the second service period begins, rather than waiting another seven years. Laban has secured fourteen years of labor but disbursed both daughters upfront — he has taken the risk that Jacob might leave after receiving Rachel without completing the second term.
29: (Laban gave his servant Bilhah to his daughter Rachel as her attendant.) — Parallel to Zilpah given to Leah (Genesis 29:24), Laban provides Bilhah as Rachel's attendant and household servant within the new marital arrangement. Bilhah will become the mother of Dan and Naphtali through Rachel's surrogate arrangement in ch.Genesis 30:3-8. The two attendants — Zilpah with Leah, Bilhah with Rachel — will function as the mothers of four of Jacob's twelve sons.
30: "His love for Rachel was greater than his love for Leah. And he worked for Laban another seven years" — the narrator states plainly the emotional asymmetry that will define the household's dynamics for the next decade. The phrase "greater than his love for Leah" does not specify what Jacob's relationship with Leah was — only that it was less. In the ANE context of polygynous households, differential love was a recognized social problem that law codes sometimes attempted to address (e.g., Deuteronomy 21:15-17 addresses the rights of the firstborn son of an unloved wife).
31: "When the LORD saw that Leah was not loved, he enabled her to conceive, but Rachel remained childless" — YHWH's intervention in the wives' fertility follows the pattern established with Sarah (Genesis 21:1) and Rebekah (Genesis 25:21): the barrenness of the beloved or covenant-significant wife, and the opening of the womb as divine act. Here the pattern is inverted: the unloved wife is given fertility as divine compensation, while the loved wife remains barren. The divine response to Leah's situation is a form of covenant justice — YHWH sees what is hidden from human view and intervenes to correct the social imbalance.
32: "She named him Reuben, for she said, 'It is because the LORD has seen my misery. Surely my husband will love me now'" — the naming speech encodes both the etymology and the theological significance. Rĕʾûbēn is parsed as rāʾāh... bĕʿānyî (he has seen my affliction) — the seeing of YHWH. Alternatively, the name reads as rĕʾû bēn (see, a son!). Both meanings are in play. Leah's hope — "surely my husband will love me now" (yĕʾehāḇanî ʾîšî) — establishes the emotional register that all four of her naming speeches will inhabit: she is giving sons in the hope of earning Jacob's affection.
33: "Because the LORD heard that I am not loved, he gave me this one too. So she named him Simeon" — Šimʿôn from šāmaʿ (to hear): YHWH has heard. The shift from sight (Reuben) to hearing (Simeon) traces two of YHWH's primary modes of engagement with human suffering — he both sees and hears the afflicted. The pattern mirrors Hagar's experience in Genesis 16:7-13, where YHWH both heard (šāmaʿ) her crying and appeared to her. Leah identifies herself as being in an analogous position to the other marginalized women in these narratives.
34: "Now at last my husband will become attached to me, because I have borne him three sons. So he was named Levi" — Lēwî from lāwāh (to cling, to be attached, to join): "now my husband will cling/attach to me." The clan of Levi — Israel's priestly tribe — receives its name from the verb of attachment. The irony of the name will become apparent in later history: the Levites will be "attached" not to a territorial allotment but to YHWH himself (Numbers 18:2-4). Leah's prayer for marital attachment becomes the name of the tribe that will be uniquely attached to divine service.
35: "This time I will praise the LORD. So she named him Judah. Then she stopped having children" — Yĕhûḏāh from yādāh (to praise, to give thanks): "this time I will praise YHWH." The fourth son's naming speech is the only one not oriented toward Jacob's affection — Leah turns instead to pure divine praise. The shift is theologically significant: after three sons named in the context of seeking her husband's love, the fourth is named in praise of YHWH alone. Judah will become the covenant tribe — the line of David and of Christ — and his name embeds the act of praise that becomes Israel's covenant response to YHWH.
